verdict_reasoning: C3 is canonically a secreted plasma complement component synthesized by hepatocytes. However, upon activation, C3 is cleaved to C3b which is deposited onto target cell surfaces via a covalent thioester bond to hydroxyl/amine groups on surface proteins and carbohydrates — a wash-resistant covalent attachment. This is the basis for opsonization and the canonical mechanism for anti-C3/C3b therapeutics (e.g., pegcetacoplan targets C3; surface-deposited C3b/iC3b/C3d are recognized by complement receptors). Thus the protein body reaches the outer leaflet of target cells via covalent post-translational tethering, satisfying stable_surface_attachment.
